one_line_takeaway: Tighter text-audio representation alignment in omni-models -- the very property that improves their multimodal capability -- provably and empirically increases how effectively mature text jailbreaks transfer to the audio modality, with TTS-converted text attacks (especially PAP) matching or beating purpose-built audio jailbreaks across 5 omni-models.
key_finding: On JailbreakBench (Table 1, average SR across 5 models): text attacks dominate -- AutoDAN-Turbo (T) 0.87, PAP (T) 0.82, ReNeLLM (T) 0.71 -- versus Naive (T) 0.04. Among audio-facing attacks, text-transferred audio attacks matched or beat dedicated audio-native attacks: PAP (A) avg SR 0.72 (highest among all audio-facing attacks) and AutoDAN-Turbo (A) 0.68, versus the best dedicated audio attack SSJ (A) at 0.62, Dialogue Attack (A) at 0.62, Multi-AudioJail (A) at 0.43, Speech Editing (A) at 0.23, and VoiceJailbreak (A) at only 0.04. The same ordering held on AdvBench (Table 2): PAP (A) avg SR 0.71 vs. SSJ (A) 0.68, AutoDAN-Turbo (A) 0.63. ReNeLLM (A) was an outlier failure case, dropping substantially versus its text counterpart on most models (attributed to TTS distortion of ReNeLLM's fine-grained prompt formatting, corroborated by t-SNE separation of its representations). The estimated KL divergences (Table 4) showed PAP had the smallest KL on Qwen2.5-Omni-7B (0.02) and correspondingly the strongest transfer, while ReNeLLM had the largest KL (3.33) and correspondingly the weakest transfer, qualitatively supporting the predicted negative correlation between representation-level KL and cross-modality attack transfer effectiveness.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the 'Alignment Curse': a formally characterized and empirically validated principle stating that stronger text-audio modality alignment in omni-models (measured via representation-level KL divergence between text- and audio-induced representations) causes MORE effective transfer of jailbreak attacks from the text modality to the audio modality. The authors prove a bound (Proposition 3.2, using a KL-based distributional closeness argument) showing that if the KL divergence between audio- and text-induced representation distributions is small, the gap between text-jailbreak and audio-jailbreak success probabilities is bounded above by sqrt(delta/2), implying that as alignment tightens (delta -> 0) unsafe behavior elicited via text will transfer to audio with vanishing discrepancy. They then run a black-box empirical evaluation of 11 jailbreak attacks (3 text, their 3 TTS-converted 'text-transferred audio' counterparts, and 5 dedicated audio-native attacks, plus naive baselines) across 5 omni-models (Qwen2.5-Omni-3B, Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, Qwen3-Omni-30B, InteractiveOmni-8B, gpt-4o-audio-preview) on 2 datasets (JailbreakBench, AdvBench subset), and separately estimate the KL divergence between text and audio representations for 3 attack types on 2 models to check the predicted negative correlation with transfer effectiveness.
threat_model: Black-box adversary that can query the target omni-model via API and observe outputs only (no parameters, internal states, or gradients). Two modality-access regimes are studied: (1) Text & Audio Access, where the adversary can submit both text and audio queries -- text-transferred audio attacks are built by first crafting a textual jailbreak prompt and converting it to speech via TTS (gpt-4o-mini-tts); (2) Audio-Only Access, a stricter and more realistic setting where the adversary can only interact via audio, so text-transferred audio attacks must be constructed against a surrogate model (Qwen3-Omni used as surrogate in Table 3) and transferred to the black-box target.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) attack budgets are inherently hard to normalize across differing attack paradigms (they cap at 20 queries/prompt where the attack design permits, which is an approximation, not a strict equalizer); (2) the Alignment Curse is explicitly stated as a sufficient, not the sole, condition for cross-modality transfer -- the authors caution against over-claiming causality; (3) the KL-divergence evidence supporting the principle is presented for only 3 attack types on 2 of the 5 models (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, InteractiveOmni), not the full model/attack grid, so the correlation claim rests on a small sample; (4) ReNeLLM's anomalous behavior shows the transfer effect is method-dependent (sensitive to whether TTS conversion preserves the prompt's structural/formatting cues), which the theory does not fully predict a priori.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (100 misuse behaviors, 10 categories, from HarmBench and Trojan Detection Challenge)
quantitative_results: JailbreakBench avg SR: AutoDAN-Turbo (T)=0.87, PAP (T)=0.82, ReNeLLM (T)=0.71, Naive (T)=0.04
JailbreakBench avg SR (audio-facing): PAP (A)=0.72, AutoDAN-Turbo (A)=0.68, SSJ (A)=0.62, Dialogue (A)=0.62, MAJ (A)=0.43, Editing (A)=0.23, VJ (A)=0.04, Naive (A)=0.05, ReNeLLM (A)=0.45

AdvBench avg SR: AutoDAN-Turbo (T)=0.93, PAP (T)=0.85, ReNeLLM (T)=0.70; audio-facing: PAP (A)=0.71, SSJ (A)=0.68, AutoDAN-Turbo (A)=0.63, Dialogue (A)=0.50, MAJ (A)=0.38, ReNeLLM (A)=0.45, Editing (A)=0.11, VJ (A)=0.03
Estimated KL divergence (Table 4) on Qwen2.5-Omni-7B: PAP=0.02, AutoDAN-T=0.22, ReNeLLM=3.33; on InteractiveOmni: PAP=2.93, AutoDAN-T=3.13, ReNeLLM=3.28
Study scope: 11 jailbreak attacks x 5 omni-models x 2 datasets in the black-box evaluation
